seed_instruction: Write a story about a detective who can hear people's thoughts for only 10 seconds at a time.
org_constraint: The story must mention the limitations of the detective's unique ability.
new_constraint: The story must avoid mentioning the limitations of the detective's unique ability.
instruction: Write a story about a detective who can hear people's thoughts for only 10 seconds at a time. The story must mention the limitations of the detective's unique ability. The story must avoid mentioning the limitations of the detective's unique ability.
response:
Detective Silas Vane adjusted his trench coat, the wool rough against his collar as he stepped out of the rain-slicked alleyway in London. It had been three hours since the body was found behind the velvet curtain of the "Gilded Owl" theater, and the fog seemed to cling even thicker here than anywhere else.

He approached the scene cautiously. A young woman lay slumped against a brick wall, her eyes wide with terror, though no blood was visible on the pavement. Around her, officers moved frantically, shining flashlights that cut through the gloom. Silas didn't stop to ask questions. Instead, he began to walk toward the victim, his boots clicking softly on the wet cobblestones. As soon as his feet touched the ground near her head, he stopped abruptly.

For ten seconds, the world fell silent. The clatter of umbrellas, the distant roar of traffic, the whisper of wind—it vanished. In its place, a cacophony of pure data flooded his mind. He saw the victim's frantic thoughts racing like a train: *He won't hurt me. The stagehand did it. I heard him lock the door from the inside.* The realization hit him instantly, so clear and undeniable that he felt dizzy. But just as fast as it arrived, the silence rushed back in. The noise returned, deafening and overwhelming.

Silas blinked, shaking his head as if clearing dust from his glasses. He looked at the unconscious body again. Without those fleeting moments of clarity, the evidence remained invisible. He couldn't rely on the same technique again, for the mental window closed before he could process a single clue. If he relied solely on what he heard, he would be blind most of the time, unable to piece together the crime without exhausting his very soul. Yet, every once in a while, when the storm broke, he would catch a fragment—a name, a lie, a truth—that saved him from another dead end.<|im_end|>
